narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90—which is below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 32.50, lower than the dataset average of 36.70. Your Year 1 school decile ranking of 4th out of 10 was also under the average of about 5.7, while your Year 3 ranking of 6th was slightly above the 5.5 average. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 54.9% chance that you would fail the bar on your first attempt, tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction hinges on just a few key factors from your record that carried the most weight in the decision. The strongest positive influence pulling toward a passing prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which showed solid improvement from your earlier performance and suggested growing academic strength compared to peers. However, your LSAT score exerted a notable push toward the failure prediction, as it fell short of what the model sees as typical for bar success. Similarly, your lower Year 1 decile ranking contributed toward the risk of failure by highlighting an early struggle that weighed against you. In contrast to many successful applicants, who often enter with stronger early grades and higher LSATs, your trajectory showed promise later on but started from a tougher spot. While your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of students (over 92%), it didn't sway the outcome much here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, as that could significantly shift future predictions in your favor. Strengthening any weak areas from your early undergrad years through additional coursework or explanations in reapplications might also help demonstrate resilience. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps, so persistence tailored to your strengths—like that Year 3 uptick—could open doors next time.
